category: Footer navigation keywords mistaken for product attributes

consistency.notes: All non-null, non-empty label values are supported by the page text. 'coffee.roaster_country' is supported indirectly via 'Portland, Oregon' implying United States. Enum-style fields are also supported: page_type matches a coffee product page; is_specialty_coffee is supported by the detailed single-origin specialty-style listing; is_blend=false follows from single origin/producer/variety presentation; is_espresso=false and is_decaf=false are explicitly stated; availability 'in_stock' is explicit.

realism.reasons:
This reads very plausibly like a real roaster product page. The structure is strong: title, country, price, size selector, stock/roast schedule, tasting notes, producer/origin details, process/variety/altitude/harvest, descriptive copy, brew recommendation, and footer/shop chrome all match common specialty coffee ecommerce layouts. The voice is believable for a modern specialty roaster: concise, origin-forward, with light technical language like 'day lot,' 'clean fermentation,' 'development for sweetness and clarity,' and a practical brew ratio. The origin data is coherent: Huila/Palestina, Pink Bourbon, washed process, high elevation, and flavor notes like white peach, panela, and orange blossom fit the profile.

The variant block is also fairly realistic. IDs, SKUs, variant titles, per-size names, and pricing structure are all sensible. 250g at $21 and 2 lb at $70 is within a believable specialty pricing range, and 2 lb rendered as 907.185g is exactly the kind of machine-derived precision that can appear in backend exports.

Minor tells keep it from a 5. 'Harvest: July–September 2025' is slightly suspicious depending on current sales timing, since many real pages either omit the year, use crop year more explicitly, or avoid future-looking harvest windows unless pre-release context is given. The explanatory sentence about espresso/decaf footer links feels a bit too self-conscious and tailored to the prompt rather than naturally commercial copy. 'Default_for_inference' in the variant data is not something a customer-facing html-to-text rendering would usually expose. Still, aside from those small synthetic hints, it is highly plausible.

label.coffee.producer_or_farm: Ana Maria Gomez, Finca El Mirador
label.coffee.sensory_text:
Tasting notes: white peach, panela, orange blossom

The result is a structured, high-sweetness cup with peach-like acidity and a panela finish.
label.coffee.producer_text: This lot comes from Finca El Mirador, a small hillside farm outside Palestina in Huila. Ana Maria Gomez separates her Pink Bourbon cherries by day lot before a long, clean fermentation and full washing in cool mountain water.
